species: Full Elk Herd + Fall Storm + Bald Eagle -- convergent apex natural drama, river valley, multi-species
scientific_name: Cervus canadensis (elk, Family Cervidae) + Fall Pacific storm system + Haliaeetus leucocephalus (Bald Eagle, Family Accipitridae). Multi-species convergence. River valley east-slope Cascades transition zone.
sound_type: Three-stream convergent natural drama: (1) Full elk herd bugling at rut maximum -- multiple bulls competing simultaneously, cows and calves responding, valley-amplified spatial acoustic layering; (2) Pacific fall storm system -- approaching wind in the conifer canopy (Douglas-fir roar, ponderosa hiss, cedar shudder), valley wind, distant thunder with valley wall reverberation (3-5 sec echo trains), precipitation onset (rain on leaves, river surface acoustic activation); (3) Bald Eagle vocalizing on thermal overhead -- thin descending kek-kek-kek call at 2,000-8,000 Hz, high-frequency counterpoint above ground-level drama. Full acoustic architecture: elk 80-1,200+ Hz episodic (3-6 sec bugles, 30-120 sec gaps); storm broadband continuous (50-2,000 Hz wind, 20-500 Hz thunder); eagle punctuate high-frequency (2,000-8,000 Hz). Three spectral regions with minimal masking. River valley amplifies and reflects all three.
ecosystem: River valley, east-slope Cascades transition zone. Valley corridor with forested ridges (Douglas-fir, ponderosa pine, western red cedar). River providing continuous acoustic background and salmon-run ecological attractor for eagles. Valley walls as acoustic reflectors. South-facing slopes generating thermals for eagle thermal soaring. Elevation: 500-1,500 meters above sea level, typical for east-Cascades river valleys (Methow, Entiat, Wenatchee, Naches drainages).
season: Fall storm, early October. Peak elk rut (September-October Washington). Pacific storm season onset. Eagle fall migration from BC and Alaska south along river valleys. October ecological calendar alignment: rut + storms + eagle movement occur simultaneously by ecological calendar, not by staging.
family: Multi-species convergence: Cervidae (Cervus canadensis) + Pacific storm meteorological system + Accipitridae (Haliaeetus leucocephalus). All three components present year-round in the region; convergence maximized in October fall transition period.
milestone: THE FINAL ASCENT OPENS. First step of SPS engine's final escalation ramp (341 E=8, 342 E=8, 343 E=9, 344 E=10 -- FIRST E=10 IN SPS ENGINE HISTORY). E=8 through three-stream convergence: three natural sources at different frequency ranges and temporal scales converging in a river valley that acoustically amplifies all three. Paired with Pearl Jam heavy (S36-341, E=8): symmetric convergence on both tracks -- both entries reach E=8 through multi-source convergence.

coordinates.description: Representative: Entiat River Valley, Chelan County, Washington. 47.80°N 120.55°W. East-slope Cascades river valley, ponderosa pine / Douglas-fir transition, active elk habitat, seasonal salmon run (fall Chinook), Bald Eagle concentration site. Methow Valley (48.5°N 120.1°W) and Naches River Valley (46.7°N 120.9°W) also representative. All three valleys: documented fall elk rut activity, Pacific storm transit corridor, Bald Eagle concentration in October.

acoustic_data.elk_herd: Multiple bulls simultaneously: each bull bugle 80-200 Hz grunt transition to 300-1,000+ Hz ascending whistle, ending 80-200 Hz grunts. Duration 3-6 seconds per bugle. Carry in valley: 3-4 km (vs. 1.5-2 km open terrain). Multi-bull simultaneous: spatial layering from different distances/directions. Herd chorus creates acoustic environment in which listener is inside the sound, not hearing it from outside. Cows: lower frequency contact calls, 200-400 Hz. Calves: high-pitched 400-800 Hz.
acoustic_data.storm_system: Wind in conifers: Douglas-fir roar 100-800 Hz sustained; ponderosa pine intermittent hiss 200-1,200 Hz; western red cedar low shudder 50-300 Hz. Thunder in valley: single crack (700-2,000 Hz peak impact) followed by reverberation decay 3-5 seconds off valley walls. Precipitation onset: rain on leaf litter 500-4,000 Hz distributed broadband; rain on river surface 1,000-8,000 Hz. Continuous broadband background 50-2,000 Hz throughout approach and onset.
acoustic_data.eagle: Bald Eagle actual call: kek-kek-kek-kek, thin descending series, 2,000-8,000 Hz. Duration: 1-3 seconds per call sequence. Temporal pattern: isolated call sequences separated by 30+ second silences. Functions: territory communication, prey alerting, pair bond maintenance. High-frequency counterpoint to ground-level components. Wingspan 1.8-2.4 m; thermal soaring: banking in rising warm air column off south-facing slopes, gaining altitude each spiral.
acoustic_data.convergence_architecture: Spectral non-overlap: elk (80-1,200 Hz), storm (50-2,000 Hz broader, dominant 100-800 Hz), eagle (2,000-8,000 Hz). Three components audible simultaneously in distinct frequency regions without masking. Temporal layering: storm continuous, elk episodic, eagle punctuate. River valley amplifies and reflects elk 3-4 km, amplifies thunder 3-5 sec echo trains, channels wind directionally. E=8 through three-stream convergence, not single-source apex.

nodes.title: THE FINAL ASCENT OPENS -- E=8 First Step
nodes.description: SPS engine ramp 341-344: E=8 (elk+storm+eagle) → E=8 (sea lion colony storm) → E=9 (peregrine through tern colony) → E=10 (multi-species polyrhythmic apex, FIRST E=10 IN ENGINE HISTORY). The escalation begins here.

nodes.title: Three-Stream Convergence at E=8
nodes.description: Elk herd (Cervidae) + fall storm (meteorological) + Bald Eagle (Accipitridae) in river valley. Three spectral regions: 80-1,200 Hz / 50-2,000 Hz broadband / 2,000-8,000 Hz. Three temporal scales: episodic / continuous / punctuate. Valley amplifies all three. E=8 through convergence, not through single-source apex.

nodes.title: October Ecological Calendar -- Natural Alignment
nodes.description: Elk rut peaks September-October. Pacific storm season begins October. Eagles concentrate on salmon runs October. Three separate ecological and meteorological cycles align in October in Cascades river valleys. The convergence is not staged -- it is the natural consequence of the ecological calendar.

nodes.title: River Valley as Acoustic Amplifier
nodes.description: Valley walls reflect and concentrate elk bugles (3-4 km carry vs. 2 km open), extend thunder reverberation (3-5 sec echo trains), channel wind directionally. River provides continuous background acoustic surface. Valley corridor concentrates all three sources. The geography is the acoustic instrument.

cross_references.relationship: E=8 + E=8 = E=16. FINAL ASCENT OPENS. Symmetric convergence: Pearl Jam (five musicians from multiple predecessors converging into one unit) + Elk+Storm+Eagle (three natural sources converging in one valley). Both reach E=8 through multi-source convergence.

340 closes the metal summit at E=9 through single-source behavioral apex (one orca). 341 opens the final ascent at E=8 through multi-source convergence (three species/systems). The transition from concentration to convergence as the mechanism of high energy.

341 is the convergence that opens the ramp. 344 is the imagined synthesis that closes it at E=10. Both are multi-source convergences; 344 is the maximum imaginable synthesis of all SPS categories simultaneously.
